Code fragment: Result<Record> result = context.select().from(USER).fetch();
Explanation: Using `select()` without specifying columns fetches all columns from the USER table, which wastes resources if only ID and FIRSTNAME are needed. Replace with `context.select(USER.ID, USER.FIRSTNAME).from(USER).fetch()` to explicitly select only the required columns.

Code fragment: UserRecord userRecord = context.fetchOne(USER, USER.ID.eq(id));
Explanation: Using `fetchOne(USER, ...)` fetches all columns of the USER table into a UserRecord, even though only the record's existence and delete capability are needed. Consider using `context.deleteFrom(USER).where(USER.ID.eq(id)).execute()` directly if you only need to delete the record.

Code fragment: UserRecord userRecord = context.fetchOne(USER, USER.ID.eq(id));
Explanation: Using `fetchOne(USER, ...)` fetches all columns of the USER table into a UserRecord, but the record data is never used—only its existence is checked. Consider using `context.fetchExists(selectFrom(USER).where(USER.ID.eq(id)))` to check existence, or remove the check entirely since the update will simply affect zero rows if the ID doesn't exist.

Code fragment: Result<Record> result = context.select().from(User.USER).fetch();
Explanation: Using `select()` without specifying columns fetches all columns from the USER table, which can retrieve unnecessary data and impact performance. Specify only the required columns explicitly, e.g., `context.select(USER.ID, USER.FIRSTNAME, USER.LASTNAME, ...).from(User.USER).fetch()`.
